data.title: Enphase leads clean energy names higher; wins upgrades from Citi, Goldman
data.description:
Enphase Energy (ENPH) +12.5% in Thursday's trading as investors moved into riskier stock sectors such as alternative energy following President Trump's reversal of plans to impose tariffs linked to Greenland, which had caused steep market losses earlier in the week.

Several other clean energy names also boasted big gains, including Plug Power (PLUG) +16.9%, Sunrun (RUN) +8.4%, FuelCell Energy (FCEL) +7.2%, Array Technologies (ARRY) +6.1%, Shoals Technologies (SHLS) +6%, Canadian Solar (CSIQ) +6%, NuScale Power (SMR) +5.6%, Nano Nuclear Energy (NNE) +5.3%, Nextpower (NXT) +5.3%, SolarEdge Technologies (SEDG) +4.7%, Eos Energy Enterprises (EOSE) +4.5%, Fluence Energy (FLNC) +4%.

Citi upgraded Enphase (ENPH) to Neutral from Sell with a $37 price target, as analyst Vikram Bagri sees risks skewed more to the upside at current levels, and Q1 expectations have been appropriately de-risked while full-year expectations appear to be within reach.

Enphase's (ENPH) market share has stabilized in California and could increase with new competitively priced storage launch vs. Powerwall, and European Union price cuts appear to be eliciting demand response, Bagri said, adding that the stock is unloved, "ranking as the least crowded long and most crowded short in our coverage, which could amplify any positive developments."

Earlier this week, Goldman Sachs analyst Brian Lee upgraded Enphase (ENPH) to Buy from Neutral with a $45 PT, pointing to improved confidence in growth opportunities while recent safe harboring activity should push Q1 2026 revenue above current consensus estimates.

Enphase (ENPH) likely benefited from stronger than expected volume demand in Q4 2025 on the back of market share gains and strong year-end follow-through from the cash/loan customer end market ahead of 25D tax credit expiration; Lee sees the company poised to enter 2026 with some momentum, while the stock's valuation remains near all-time lows and risk-reward looks more favorable, especially considering investor sentiment remains sharply skewed negative based on elevated short interest levels, despite potential for a near-term beat vs. consensus.

data.title: Heritage Financial outlines margin improvement potential near 4% with Olympic merger integration
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Heritage Financial Corporation (HFWA) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Bryan McDonald, CEO, reported that "our improving net interest margin and a shift in our loan mix benefiting the provision expense drove earnings higher in the fourth quarter." McDonald stated that adjusted diluted earnings per share was up 18% versus last quarter and up 29% versus the fourth quarter of 2024. He highlighted that regulatory and shareholder approval for the pending merger with Olympic Bancorp has been secured, and closure is planned by the end of January. McDonald emphasized, "Their addition to the Heritage franchise will add to the profitability of our operations and better position our company for growth in the Puget Sound market."
  • Donald Hinson, CFO, stated, "Total loan balances increased $14 million in Q4. Yields on the loan portfolio were 5.54%, which is 1 basis point higher than Q3... Total deposits increased to $63 million in Q4. This increase was due primarily to a $100 million increase in interest-bearing demand deposits. The cost of interest-bearing demand deposits decreased to 1.83% from 1.89% in the prior quarter." Hinson added, "Net interest income increased $1 million or 1.7% from the prior quarter due primarily to a higher net interest margin. The net interest margin increased to 3.72% from 3.64% in the prior quarter and from 3.36% in the fourth quarter of 2024." He also pointed out that noninterest expense decreased $132,000 from the prior quarter due mostly to lower merger-related expenses.
  • Tony Chalfant, Chief Credit Officer, reported, "Nonaccrual loans totaled $21 million at year-end, and we do not hold any OREO. This represents 0.44% of total loans and compares to 0.37% at the end of the third quarter." Chalfant noted a decline in criticized loans by $6.6 million during the quarter, with a 24% increase in substandard loans primarily from the downgrade of 2 C&I relationships totaling just under $30 million.

Outlook

  • Management expects margin improvement to continue over the next year or two, excluding the merger, with Hinson stating, "So overall, I think that -- and this is all without the merger, right, that's going to occur. So just on the -- on the legacy Heritage side, we expect to see margin improvement to continue over the next year or 2."
  • On the combined entity, Hinson said, "I think we're going to get a nice bump in margin where we could potentially get near that 4% range by the end of the year."
  • McDonald indicated, "Looking ahead to 2026, we expect to resume loan growth at more historical levels as we are through the period of elevated loan payoffs, and we expect net advances to move back to a positive position."
  • On operating expenses, Hinson projected, "Probably a run rate for Q2 and Q3 will probably be in the 56s... and then we'll get more of our cost savings in Q4. And the core will probably be down more in the $54 million range after that."

Financial Results

  • Net interest income increased $1 million or 1.7% from the prior quarter, driven by a higher net interest margin, which rose to 3.72% from 3.64% in Q3 and from 3.36% in Q4 2024.
  • The company recognized a reversal of provision for credit losses in the amount of $814,000 in Q4, attributed to a shift in the loan portfolio mix.
  • Borrowing balances decreased to $20 million at year-end from $138 million at the end of Q3, reflecting the use of investment portfolio cash flows and deposit growth to pay down borrowings.
  • Noninterest expense decreased $132,000 from the prior quarter, primarily due to lower merger-related expenses.
  • All regulatory capital ratios remain above well-capitalized thresholds, with the TCE ratio at 10.1%, up from 9.8% in the prior quarter.

Q&A

  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Asked about margin outlook and the impact of adjustable rate opportunities. Hinson responded that despite three rate cuts, "we were still able to slightly grow our loan yields in Q4" and anticipates further margin improvement if rate cuts pause. Regarding the Olympic merger, Hinson stated, "we're going to get a nice bump in margin where we could potentially get near that 4% range by the end of the year."
  • Adam Kroll, Piper Sandler: Asked about loan payoffs and growth by industry/geography. McDonald confirmed that most anticipated payoffs occurred and expects payoff volume to moderate by about one-third. On operating expense growth, Hinson provided guidance on merger-related expenses and anticipated run rates.
  • Jackson Laurent, Stephens: Inquired about competitive dynamics and deposit costs. McDonald said, "it continues to be a really competitive market for the clients that we're going after," but competition is not outsized. Hinson explained that deposit costs are expected to continue decreasing.
  • Liam Coohill, Raymond James: Asked about interest-bearing deposit growth and credit trends. McDonald attributed deposit growth to relationship banking and recent team expansions. Chalfant stated that C&I downgrades were not systemic and do not indicate a broader trend.
  • Kelly Motta, KBW: Sought insight on efficiency ratio improvements post-merger. Hinson expects the efficiency ratio to decline over time, primarily driven by revenue growth, while McDonald highlighted the significant potential upside from low-cost deposits and leveraging opportunities from the Olympic merger.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a generally positive but probing tone, focusing on margin outlook, merger integration, expenses, and deposit trends. Questions were detailed and sought clarity on forward-looking metrics and operational strategy.
  • Management's tone was confident during prepared remarks, using phrases such as "we are pleased with our solid performance" and "we believe we are well positioned". During Q&A, responses were measured, with both Hinson and McDonald providing direct answers and clarifying uncertainties, indicating confidence in the company's trajectory and merger integration.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analyst and management sentiment remained stable, with a continued focus on operational improvement and merger benefits. Management's language reflected heightened confidence in margin expansion and operational efficiency.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Margin improved in Q4, rising to 3.72% from 3.64% in Q3. Net interest income growth moderated compared to the previous quarter.
  • The company reversed provision expense for credit losses in Q4, contrasting with a provision expense in Q3.
  • Noninterest expense decreased in Q4, reflecting reduced merger-related costs, while Q3 saw an increase due to higher compensation and professional services.
  • Deposit growth continued, though slightly lower than Q3's increase. Loan balances grew modestly in Q4 after being flat in Q3.
  • Management's confidence in merger integration and margin improvement increased, with more specific forward-looking projections for margin and efficiency ratio following the Olympic merger.
  • Analyst focus shifted more toward post-merger operational synergies and future efficiency gains compared to Q3's focus on organic loan growth and deposit competition.

Risks and Concerns

  • Chalfant noted an increase in substandard loans due to two C&I relationship downgrades but emphasized these remain at manageable levels and are "in line with our longer-term historical performance."
  • McDonald acknowledged persistent competition in both loans and deposits but did not view it as above normal levels.
  • Hinson flagged the need for continued focus on integration costs and efficiency following the Olympic merger.
  • Management expressed confidence in proactive credit management and stated that early identification of problem credits has led to low loan losses.

Final Takeaway

Heritage Financial Corporation closed out 2025 with notable improvements in net interest margin, a reversed provision for credit losses, and ongoing deposit growth. The pending Olympic Bancorp merger, expected to close by the end of January, is set to drive further margin expansion with management signaling potential to approach a 4% net interest margin by year-end. Credit quality remains solid with manageable risk trends, and efficiency improvements are anticipated as the merged entity leverages scale and cost synergies. Management expresses confidence in continued operational and financial progress as the company enters 2026, supported by disciplined credit underwriting and strategic growth initiatives.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: J.B. Hunt Transport Services raises dividend by 2.3%
data.description:
• J.B. Hunt Transport Services (JBHT) declared $0.45/share quarterly dividend, 2.3% increase from prior dividend of $0.44.
  • Forward yield 0.86%

  • See JBHT Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Allied Gold gains amid takeover speculation
data.description:
• Allied Gold (AAUC) rose 3.5% at least partly amid takeover speculation. 
  • Allied Gold (AAUC) has attracted takeover interest, according to traders, who cited a Betaville "uncooked" alert that was circulating on Thursday. There's speculation that the group circling Allied Gold may be Asian. 
  • Shares of Allied Gold (AAUC) have risen 23% over the past month and 328% over the past year.

data.title: Procter & Gamble maintains 2%–4% organic sales growth outlook as U.S. interventions ramp up
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Procter & Gamble (PG) Q2 2026

Management View

  • CEO Shailesh Jejurikar emphasized confidence in the company's interventions and investments to improve near-term performance, noting, "We are confident the interventions and investments we are making now will improve our near-term performance, strong innovation supported by sharper consumer communication and retail execution." Jejurikar highlighted success stories such as Greater China Baby Care, where Pampers Prestige has driven double-digit organic sales growth over the past 18 months, and Mexico fabric enhancers, which achieved double-digit growth through product innovation and sharper brand communication.

  • The CEO announced a longer-term reinvention of P&G, describing the initiative as "the next important phase of constructive disruption that will create and extend our competitive advantages in each element of our strategy." He outlined the company's intent to leverage data and technology, referencing the buildout of a data lake and AI capabilities, and stated, "We will double down on productivity with multiyear visibility to fund capabilities, innovation and demand creation and to mitigate cost headwinds while delivering financial results at the levels you and we expect."

  • CFO Andre Schulten remarked, "As we expected, second quarter top line results heavily reflect underlying market trends and impacts from base period dynamics." Schulten noted that the quarter was anticipated to be the softest of the fiscal year, with confidence in stronger growth ahead. He reported that "organic sales were in line with prior year," and "core earnings per share were $1.88, in line with prior year." Schulten also shared that P&G returned $4.8 billion of cash to shareowners in the quarter.

Outlook

  • CFO Schulten reiterated that fiscal 2026 guidance ranges are unchanged, continuing to expect organic sales growth of in line to plus 4%. He projected global market growth for the company’s portfolio footprint at around 2% on a value basis and expects stronger growth in the U.S. as interventions take hold. The core EPS outlook remains in line to plus 4% versus prior year, or a range of $6.83 to $7.09 per share. Schulten stated, "We expect to pay around $10 billion in dividends and to repurchase approximately $5 billion in common stock, combined a plan to return roughly $15 billion of cash to shareowners in fiscal '26."

  • The outlook includes approximately $500 million before tax in higher costs from tariffs and a core effective tax rate of 20% to 21%. Adjusted free cash flow productivity is forecast between 85% and 90%.

Financial Results

  • P&G's organic sales were flat year over year, with volume down 1 point, pricing up 1 point, and mix flat. Seven of ten product categories held or grew organic sales. Notably, Family Care was down approximately 10% due to base period dynamics. Excluding Family Care, organic sales rose 1%. North America organic sales declined by 2%, while Latin America and the Europe Enterprise Market region grew 8% and 6%, respectively. Greater China organic sales advanced 3%.

  • Core gross margin was down 50 basis points, and core operating margin decreased by 70 basis points. Productivity improvement was 270 basis points, and adjusted free cash flow productivity was 88%.

Q&A

  • Lauren Lieberman, Barclays: Asked about the rationale behind expected near-term acceleration and the company's reinvention. CFO Schulten pointed to strong performance outside the U.S. and said, "The main element here, I think, is the fundamental execution of the same interventions we made outside of the U.S. earlier." Jejurikar described growth opportunities and a unique chance to leverage shifts in media, technology, and consumer demographics.

  • Stephen Powers, Deutsche Bank: Sought more detail on where acceleration is expected and the transformation timeline. Schulten outlined that "the innovation interventions, the commercial interventions, the execution focus is consistently applied across every part of the portfolio," with Family Care, Baby Care, and Fem Care expected to benefit most in the near term. Jejurikar estimated, "by the time we really get the future evenly distributed, I think we are talking 12 to 18 months."

  • Christopher Carey, Wells Fargo: Asked about investment levels and cost structure. Schulten explained, "Many of the investments have been made over the last decade...The investment to activate the technology, specifically around the innovation capabilities, the media capabilities won't be significant."

  • Dara Mohsenian, Morgan Stanley: Inquired about U.S. priorities. Jejurikar responded that key interventions include adapting to the new media landscape, innovating with a stronger core and bigger adjacencies, and delivering consumer value without price changes.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts frequently pressed for clarity on the pace of U.S. recovery, margin trajectory, and market share, with a slightly cautious tone and focus on execution risk and competitive headwinds.

  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and responses, repeatedly stressing confidence in interventions, innovation, and reinvention. Schulten used phrases like "we believe" and "we are confident," while Jejurikar emphasized, "We know what we need to do, and we are excited by the opportunities ahead."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remains probing, but management's tone has shifted from cautious optimism to a stronger emphasis on strategic reinvention and technology integration.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for organic sales growth (in line to plus 4%), core EPS range, and financial targets are unchanged from Q1.

  • Market share declined 20 basis points this quarter, compared to a 30 basis point decline in Q1. Management confidence has increased regarding U.S. interventions and the timeline for benefits, while reinforcing the need for sharper execution and integration of technology and data.

  • Analysts continue to focus on U.S. growth, innovation, and promotional activity, with persistent questions on margin outlook and share recovery.

  • Management's language has evolved to highlight a more comprehensive reinvention and accelerated integration of data-driven strategies.

Risks and Concerns

  • Schulten acknowledged "a challenging start to the fiscal year with softer consumer markets, aggressive competition and a dynamic geopolitical landscape."

  • Management noted that the outlook does not anticipate significant currency, commodity, or supply chain disruptions.

  • Base period dynamics, particularly in the U.S., have weighed on results, and market share loss remains a concern, though interventions are underway.

Final Takeaway

Procter & Gamble's leadership emphasized a confident outlook for the remainder of fiscal 2026, maintaining guidance and outlining a major reinvention initiative leveraging data and technology. Interventions in innovation and sharper execution are expected to drive performance improvements, particularly in the U.S., as the company aims for stronger growth and market share gains in the second half of the year. Management is focused on operational rigor, productivity, and building competitive advantage for the long term, while navigating a challenging consumer and competitive landscape.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: GE Aerospace targets $7.10–$7.40 EPS and $8B–$8.4B free cash flow for 2026 while expanding LEAP MRO capacity
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: General Electric Company (GE) Q4 2025

Management View

  • H. Culp, Chairman & CEO, opened by stating, "2025 was an outstanding year for GE Aerospace as we made operational progress, delivered on our financial commitments and continued to invest in our future." Orders increased 74% in the fourth quarter, with revenue up 20% and EPS up 19% to $1.57. Free cash flow grew 15%.
  • For the full year, orders were up 32%, revenue increased 21%, operating profit grew $1.8 billion, and free cash flow rose $1.5 billion. CES profit increased 26% to $8.9 billion and DPT profit increased 22% to $1.3 billion.
  • The company is investing in capacity and technology, with over $1 billion earmarked for MRO investments, including $500 million dedicated to LEAP. This aims to double LEAP internal capacity and support a growing installed base expected to triple between 2024 and 2030.
  • H. Culp highlighted organizational changes: "We're expanding CES to include T&O, now led by Mohamed Ali... We're also elevating our customer-facing teams led by Jason Tonich, now reporting directly to me." Russell Stokes will retire in July 2026 after 29 years.
  • CFM56 and LEAP shop turnaround times improved by over 10% year-over-year in Q4, and LEAP internal shop visit volume grew 27%.
  • Major customer wins included Riyadh Air’s commitment for 120 LEAP-1A engines, flydubai’s selection of 60 GEnx engines, Pegasus Airlines’ commitment for up to 300 LEAP-1B engines, and Delta’s order for 30 Boeing 787s powered by GEnx engines.
  • Rahul Ghai, Senior VP & CFO, stated, "Fourth quarter orders were up 74% with CES up 76% and DPT up 61%. Revenue was up 20%, led by CES services up 31%. Operating profit was $2.3 billion, up 14%." EPS for the quarter was $1.57, up 19%.

Outlook

  • For 2026, H. Culp stated, "We’re poised for another year of substantial revenue, EPS and cash growth." Revenue is expected to be up low double digits, with commercial services up mid-teens. Operating profit is projected at $9.85 billion to $10.25 billion, and EPS guidance is $7.10 to $7.40, up nearly 15% at the midpoint. Free cash flow is expected at $8 billion to $8.4 billion, with conversion remaining well above 100%.
  • CES guidance includes mid-teens revenue growth, internal shop visit and spare parts revenue both up mid-teens, and LEAP internal shop visits expected to grow 25%. Equipment revenue is expected up mid- to high teens, and LEAP deliveries up 15%. CES profit guidance is $9.6 billion to $9.9 billion. DPT revenue is guided to mid- to high single-digit growth with profit of $1.55 billion to $1.65 billion.
  • Corporate costs and eliminations are expected at $1.2 billion to $1.3 billion, reflecting lower interest income, AI investments, and higher eliminations. CapEx is expected at roughly 3% of sales.

Financial Results

  • Full-year operating profit increased 25% to $9.1 billion, with margins expanding 70 basis points to 21.4%. EPS increased 38% to $6.37. Free cash flow reached $7.7 billion, up 24% year-over-year with conversion over 110%.
  • CES Q4 profit was $2.3 billion, up 5%, with services up 31% and internal shop visit revenue up 30%. Spare parts sales were up over 25%. Equipment revenue grew 7%, with engine deliveries up 40% including LEAP up 49%.
  • DPT Q4 revenue grew 13%, defense units were down 7%, but sequentially, defense engine shipments marked the third consecutive quarter of growth. DPT profit increased 5%, margins were 8.9%.
  • The company is updating segment reporting, moving $1.4 billion of revenue and several hundred million in profit from CES to DPT to align with organizational changes.

Q&A

  • John Godyn, Citigroup: Asked about commercial aftermarket momentum and services growth for 2026. H. Culp responded, "We haven't seen anything here at the beginning of the year that gives us pause relative to the tailwinds... We feel like we have another very strong commercial services year supporting the aftermarket." Rahul Ghai added, "We expect both shop visits and spare parts to be up kind of in the same range as mid-teens as the overall services growth."
  • Myles Walton, Wolfe Research: Asked about LEAP OE profitability and supply chain investments. H. Culp stated, "We have improved over the course of 2025, our visibility further out and deeper into the supply chain... I feel very good that with the body work we put in for 2025, we're poised to step up again." Ghai confirmed, "We expect LEAP OE to be profitable in 2026 as per our prior plans."
  • Douglas Harned, Bernstein: Asked about improvement in turnaround times. H. Culp explained, "It's really driven by 2 things: one, material availability, and two, efficient execution of our standard work on the shop floor."
  • Scott Deuschle, Deutsche Bank: Asked about GE9X losses and earnings cadence. Ghai indicated, "Our losses on the 9X programs will double year-over-year. So our current guidance for '26 incorporates those losses." CES and DPT are expected to start 2026 strong.
  • Sheila Kahyaoglu, Jefferies: Inquired about CES margin drivers. Ghai outlined, "Strong services growth here... OE shipments are increasing, the spare engine ratio gradually comes down... and then 9X shipments and with R&D coming in as well."
  • Seth Seifman, JPMorgan: Asked about margin trajectory beyond 2026. H. Culp referenced the $10 billion operating profit milestone for 2026, with further growth expected from services and defense.
  • Ron Epstein, BofA Securities: Asked about $3 billion annual R&D spend. H. Culp detailed investments in LEAP improvements, 9X, and next-gen technology including the RISE program.
  • Gavin Parsons, UBS: Asked about CFM56 retirements. H. Culp replied, "I think on balance, '26 is probably a little bit better for us, likely in the 2% range compared, I think, to a 2% to 3% range we offered up in July."
  • Noah Poponak, Goldman Sachs: Asked about the FTAI agreement and free cash flow bridge. H. Culp said, "We have an open third-party aftermarket... customers have as much optionality as possible in how they service their fleets." Ghai noted, "Nothing abnormal here in '26."
  • Gautam Khanna, TD Cowen: Inquired about aftermarket customer behavior. H. Culp observed, "Demand environment post pandemic has been robust for the airlines... They want more and they want it faster without any compromise with respect to safety or quality."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on sustainability of aftermarket growth, margin headwinds, LEAP and 9X profitability, and capital deployment. Questions reflected interest in guidance credibility and operational levers.
  • Management’s tone during prepared remarks was confident and forward-looking, emphasizing strength in orders, backlog, and execution. In Q&A, management was detailed and occasionally defensive, particularly on margin and loss topics, using phrases like "as we previously said" and "we feel good about the trajectory."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence remains high. Analysts’ tone is consistently probing on sustainability and headwinds.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is more specific, now projecting EPS of $7.10–$7.40 and free cash flow of $8–$8.4 billion, compared to the previous quarter’s range of $6–$6.20 EPS and $7.1–$7.3 billion free cash flow.
  • Strategic focus has shifted to expanding MRO capacity for LEAP and integrating T&O into CES. Organizational changes include leadership transitions not announced last quarter.
  • Analysts continue to focus on sustainability of growth, margin pressures, and supply chain improvements, with new attention on LEAP profitability and 9X losses.
  • Key metrics such as operating profit, revenue, and backlog showed sequential and year-over-year improvement.
  • Management’s tone is consistent, maintaining confidence in growth and execution, while analysts remain focused on operational risks and profitability trends.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged ongoing supply chain constraints and the need for continued investment to meet demand.
  • GE9X losses are expected to double in 2026, impacting profit.
  • Inventory growth remains a challenge, though expected to moderate in 2026.
  • Analysts expressed concern regarding margin sustainability amid rising R&D and OE shipments.

Final Takeaway

GE Aerospace reported a strong finish to 2025 and projects another year of growth in 2026 with robust orders, a growing backlog, and expanding aftermarket capacity. The company anticipates low double-digit revenue growth, $7.10–$7.40 EPS, and $8–$8.4 billion free cash flow, supported by operational improvements, strategic investments in LEAP and MRO expansion, as well as continued focus on customer-driven execution. Management remains confident in meeting rising demand and delivering value for customers and shareholders, while mindful of supply chain risks, margin headwinds, and the need for ongoing investment in technology and capacity.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Public Service Enterprise cut at J.P. Morgan on New Jersey political uncertainty
data.description:
Public Service Enterprise Group (PEG) -0.5% in Thursday's trading as J.P. Morgan downgraded shares to Neutral from Overweight with an $85 price target, trimmed from $88, expecting persistent meaningful political and regulatory uncertainty in New Jersey, despite hopes the inauguration speech from new Governor Sherrill would ease fears related to utility regulatory stability.

While precise New Jersey plans remain unclear, JPM's Jeremy Tonet said he cannot rule out a
further deterioration in the state's regulatory environment, with risk skewing to the downside given elevated scrutiny on regulated returns and overall ratemaking components, and the analyst expects heightened affordability to effectively prevent meaningfully accretive nuclear contracting in the state, given likely political blowback.

Partially offsetting the risks, Tonet said he still sees upside from rising power prices and noted that roughly one-third of PSEG's (PEG) nuclear capacity sits in Pennsylvania, where contracts could still emerge.

While Sherrill may have secured sufficient political wins by freezing rate hikes, and the state's 180-day study might not result in any substantive changes, Tonet sees the affordability overhang
persisting for some time and limiting PEG upside - "On balance, while the known components to the EO appear manageable, we view this extended political uncertainty as inconsistent with
utility investor preferences, particularly given the pronounced likelihood for incremental negative headlines to emerge."

data.title: Convicted crypto exec Caroline Ellison released from federal custody - report
data.description:
Caroline Ellison, a key player in running Sam Bankman-Fried's crypto empire before its collapse in late 2022, was released from federal custody earlier this week after serving about 14 months in community confinement, Business Insider reported, citing a BOP spokesperson.

She was heading Alameda Research, the crypto hedge fund created by Bankman-Fried that played a central role in diverting customer funds from sister crypto exchange FTX. 

Ellison, sentenced to two years in prison with three years of probation for her role in the large-scale fraud that led to FTX's meltdown, was the key witness at Bankman-Fried's criminal trial in 2023, helping prosecutors in his conviction. Last year, Bankman-Fried was hit with a 25-year prison sentence on seven counts, including wire fraud, money laundering, and conspiracy.

Ryan Salame, former CEO of FTX's Bahamas subsidiary and the first of SBF's close associates to be sentenced, was ordered to spend 7.5 years in prison.

Business Insider said that Ellison's attorneys declined to comment.

The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission in December filed proposed final consent judgments to resolve civil cases against Ellison, as well as Gary Wang, the former chief technology head at FTX Trading, and Nishad Singh, the former FTX co-lead engineer. As part of the resolutions, Ellison agreed not to serve as an officer or director in other firms for 10 years.

data.title: CACI raises 2026 guidance to $725M free cash flow and 8%-10% revenue growth as technology portfolio expands
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CACI International Inc (CACI) Q2 2026

Management View

  • John Mengucci, President and CEO, highlighted CACI's evolution beyond traditional industry labels, focusing on expanding national security capabilities: "Through deliberate actions, informed investments and flexible and opportunistic capital deployment, we have expanded our technology portfolio to nearly 60% of total revenue." Mengucci pointed to strong second quarter results, with $138 million in free cash flow, 6% revenue growth, and an 11.8% EBITDA margin. He underscored the company’s leading position in electronic warfare, which now accounts for about $2 billion in revenue, and leadership in Agile software development.
  • Mengucci announced the acquisition of ARKA as "the latest step in our technology-driven portfolio evolution and the execution of our space market strategy." He also noted, "We are raising our fiscal 2026 guidance" due to strong first half performance and increased visibility.
  • Jeffrey MacLauchlan, Executive VP and CFO, stated, "In the second quarter, we generated revenue of $2.2 billion, representing 5.7% year-over-year growth, of which 4.5% was organic." MacLauchlan cited a year-over-year EBITDA margin increase of 70 basis points and adjusted diluted earnings per share of $6.81, up 14% from the prior year. He added, "We now expect revenue to be between $9.3 billion and $9.5 billion... EBITDA margin to be in the 11.7% to 11.8% range... and free cash flow guidance to at least $725 million."

Outlook

  • Management revised guidance upwards, now expecting free cash flow of at least $725 million, revenue growth of nearly 8% to 10%, and EBITDA margin in the 11.7% to 11.8% range for fiscal 2026. Mengucci stated, "We are raising our fiscal year 2026 guidance."
  • MacLauchlan provided detailed guidance: "We now expect revenue to be between $9.3 billion and $9.5 billion... adjusted net income guidance to be between $630 million and $645 million... adjusted EPS to between $28.25 and $28.92 per share."
  • None of the projections include any benefit from the planned acquisition of ARKA.

Financial Results

  • CACI reported revenue of $2.2 billion for Q2 2026 with 5.7% year-over-year growth and 4.5% organic growth. EBITDA margin reached 11.8%, a year-over-year increase of 70 basis points.
  • Adjusted diluted EPS was $6.81, up 14% from the prior year. Free cash flow stood at $138 million for the quarter, with DSO at 57 days.
  • Backlog grew to $33 billion, up 3% year-over-year, and funded backlog increased 7% for the same period. Q2 book-to-bill was 0.65x; trailing 12-month book-to-bill stood at 1.3x.
  • Leverage at quarter end was 2.4x net debt to trailing 12-month EBITDA, with expectations to rise to 4.3x post-ARKA acquisition before returning to the low 3s within six quarters.

Q&A

  • Gavin Parsons, UBS: Asked about the impact of higher U.S. military OPTEMPO. Mengucci responded, "Today's OPTEMPO is extremely good for CACI because it requires much of what traditional companies, frankly, don't traditionally do."
  • Parsons followed up on the bid pipeline, to which MacLauchlan explained, "The length of the shutdown... put some amount of acquisition processes sort of behind the curve. We do see that filling back up."
  • Peter Arment, Baird: Inquired about a recent protest win and reconciliation funding. Mengucci shared, "JTMS protest was officially denied last week... it's a 10-year, $1.6 billion job... that's going to help us drive our fourth quarter revenue number."
  • Colin Canfield, Cantor: Asked about federal acquisition reform and ARKA scalability. Mengucci stated, "We are very well poised to support where the government is going" and described ARKA as "a leading developer and supplier of sense and sensemaking capabilities... involved in just about every critical national security mission."
  • Christopher Barbero, JPMorgan: Asked about CACI's addressable market from reconciliation bill funding. Mengucci replied, "The reconciliation dollars is a tens of billions of dollars to our addressable market... we're looking at about a $300 billion TAM."
  • Additional questions covered topics such as margin performance, government acquisition models, and the scalability of recent acquisitions.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised questions about the pace of awards, protest outcomes, scalability of acquisitions, and margin sustainability, often with a slightly positive and constructive tone, focusing on future opportunity and risk management.
  • Management maintained a confident and assertive tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing proactive investment, strategic positioning, and operational discipline. Mengucci stated, "We are confident we will continue to drive long-term shareholder value."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was more optimistic, underpinned by raised guidance and recent contract wins, while analysts remained focused on execution risks and operational details.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • CACI raised fiscal 2026 guidance after maintaining it in Q1, reflecting increased visibility and momentum. Revenue growth targets rose from 7%-9% to nearly 8%-10%, and free cash flow guidance increased from at least $710 million to at least $725 million.
  • Margin guidance increased slightly, and management cited stronger technology portfolio contributions and successful cost controls.
  • The acquisition of ARKA was highlighted as a major new initiative, whereas the previous quarter focused on organic growth and ongoing contract wins.
  • Analysts continued to probe for risks related to government shutdowns and funding timing but shifted more attention to scalability of acquisitions and margin sustainability.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged lingering impacts from the government shutdown, delayed program timing, and slow rebound in award decisions.
  • Leverage is set to increase to 4.3x with the ARKA acquisition, but management asserted confidence in reducing it back to the low 3s within six quarters.
  • Potential risks from changes in federal acquisition regulations and shifts from cost-plus to firm fixed price models were discussed, with management stating they are well-positioned for evolving procurement models.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the pace of reconciliation funding, scalability of acquisitions, and possible risks for early-stage program cancellations, but management expressed confidence in mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

The company underscored its raised fiscal 2026 guidance, ongoing evolution toward a technology-driven business, and strong positioning in key growth markets like electronic warfare and Agile software. Management attributed improved outlook and resilience to deliberate investment, operational discipline, and proactive engagement with evolving customer needs, while highlighting the ARKA acquisition as a significant driver for future growth and shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Huntington projects 13% fee revenue growth and 11% loan growth for 2026 while advancing integration of Cadence and Veritex partnerships
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Huntington Bancshares Incorporated (HBAN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Stephen Steinour outlined Huntington’s momentum entering its 160th year, stating, “Nearly every part of Huntington is performing at a high level, creating powerful momentum as we look to the future.” He emphasized the bank’s expansion, with a presence in 21 states and the integration of Veritex and Cadence as strategic partnerships to accelerate growth. Steinour described the operating model as a “franchise-defining competitive advantage,” emphasizing local delivery of national capabilities and a moderate to low-risk profile. He cited 2025 results of 11% revenue growth, 16% adjusted EPS growth, and 290 basis points of positive operating leverage.
  • Brantley Standridge (Senior EVP and President of Consumer & Regional Banking) explained the approach to recent partnerships: “Our approach is collaborative and transparent, designed to align around our common objectives, creating a strong foundation for long-term value creation.” Standridge highlighted the rapid integration of Veritex, completed in 187 days, and projected Cadence’s systems migration midyear 2026, with cost and revenue synergies expected to accelerate thereafter.
  • CFO Zachary Wasserman reported, “For the quarter, earnings per common share was $0.30. On an adjusted basis, excluding acquisition-related expenses and other notable items, EPS was $0.37, up 9% year-over-year.” He noted 14.4% average loan growth and 5.1% quarter-over-quarter increase in average deposits. Wasserman detailed strong performance in fee income, disciplined expense management, and robust capital generation, with tangible book value up 19% year-over-year.

Outlook

  • Management expects 2026 net interest income growth between 10% and 13%, supported by 11% to 12% loan growth and 8% to 9% deposit growth. Fee revenues are projected to rise 13% to 16%. Core expenses are expected to grow 10% to 11%, with baseline operating leverage of 150 to 200 basis points. Guidance includes expected cost synergies from Veritex fully realized by Q2 and Cadence by Q4. Cadence is forecasted to add $1.85B to $1.9B in NII and $300M in fee revenue for the year. Net charge-offs are projected between 25 and 35 basis points, with losses likely at the lower end initially.
  • The guidance language emphasized continued investment in digital capabilities and expansion in new and existing markets, noting that “our current 2026 forecast for Huntington’s stand-alone growth in NII, in assets, deposits and fees generally exceeds the growth we’ve experienced in these categories in 2025.”

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter reported EPS was $0.30, with adjusted EPS of $0.37. Average loans increased 14.4% year-over-year. Excluding Veritex, organic loan growth was $10.1B for the year. Average deposits rose 5.1% quarter-over-quarter and 8.6% year-over-year. Net interest margin for the quarter was 3.15%, up 2 basis points sequentially. Fee businesses showed broad strength, with commercial payment revenues up 8% and wealth management up 10%.
  • Operating expenses increased just $7M sequentially (excluding Veritex and one-time costs), reflecting cost discipline. Adjusted operating leverage for the year reached 290 basis points. Tangible book value grew 19% over the year, with 40% of earnings returned through dividends. Net charge-offs were 24 basis points.

Q&A

  • L. Erika Penala (UBS): Asked for clarification on expense trajectory and the layering of cost saves and investments. Zachary Wasserman answered, "Fundamentally, what we see at this point is underlying Huntington expense growth in the mid-single digits, aligned to generate 1.5 to 2 points of operating leverage.” Penala also queried the partnership approach, with Standridge responding that the model “creates a high level of certainty for the colleagues of both Veritex and Cadence” and allows for rapid realization of synergies.
  • Jon Arfstrom (RBC): Requested a tighter range for Q1 expenses. Wasserman declined to give quarterly guidance, focusing instead on annual operating leverage and marginal profitability from Cadence integration.
  • Kenneth Usdin (Bernstein Autonomous): Asked for the FY25 baseline for core expenses, to which Wasserman replied, “It’s $4.871 billion, Ken.”
  • Matthew O’Connor (Deutsche Bank): Sought details on fee contributions from recent capital market deals, with Wasserman stating, “It’s coming in sort of $80 million to $90 million of expense above revenues.”
  • Manan Gosalia (Morgan Stanley): Asked about AI investment and whether 2026 is a high point for spending. Wasserman responded that investment growth is “a sustainable long-term model,” with significant AI investment included under digital and technology.
  • Multiple analysts probed for details on loan growth, cost saves, deposit betas, and revenue synergies, with management consistently emphasizing momentum, but deferring some revenue synergy guidance to future updates.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly pressing, with many seeking clarity on expense layering, synergy realization timing, and baseline guidance figures, but no indications of deep skepticism or negativity.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone during both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently reiterating “confidence” and emphasizing certainty and momentum; for example, Wasserman said, “Very, very confident” regarding the 55% efficiency ratio target.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained optimistic and focused on growth, with a slight increase in emphasis on integration execution and synergy realization.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 guidance provided more explicit details on Cadence integration, including expected NII and fee revenue contributions, compared to the prior quarter’s focus on Veritex. The 2026 outlook now projects higher growth rates in NII, loans, deposits, and fees than previously experienced in 2025.
  • Management reiterated and expanded on its cost discipline and operating leverage focus, now targeting 150 to 200 basis points in 2026, following 290 basis points achieved in 2025.
  • Analyst questions shifted from general growth momentum and credit quality (Q3) to specifics of integration, expense layering, and timing of synergies (Q4).
  • Management’s confidence in achieving revenue and cost synergies from both partnerships was more pronounced, with a clearer timeline for realization.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management recognized a competitive deposit environment but expressed confidence in “optimizing volume growth and pricing.”
  • The criticized asset ratio increased to 4.2%, primarily due to Veritex’s commercial real estate loans, but remained within historical range.
  • Integration risk was addressed, with management emphasizing rapid and collaborative decision-making to retain talent and minimize disruption.
  • Analysts raised concerns about expense run rates, timing of cost saves, and potential for additional M&A, all of which management addressed by reiterating organic growth focus and disciplined investment.

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized that Huntington’s strong 2025 results set a foundation for even greater growth in 2026, backed by substantial investments in digital, rapid integration of strategic partnerships, and a disciplined approach to costs and capital. The company expects to realize both cost and revenue synergies from Veritex and Cadence, driving outperformance in net interest income, fee revenue, and operating leverage, while maintaining a moderate risk profile and expanding its presence in high-growth markets.
